items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: When two waves interfere destructively, what happens to the energy?
items.correct: Energy is redistributed — there is constructive interference elsewhere, conserving total energy.
items.misconception: Energy is destroyed at points of destructive interference.
items.random_wrong: Energy turns into mass according to E=mc^2 when waves cancel.
items.why: Students take 'destructive' literally and assume energy is annihilated, missing that interference always involves a redistribution pattern.

items.q: If you double your distance from a point source of waves, what happens to the intensity?
items.correct: It falls to one quarter — intensity follows an inverse-square law with distance from a point source.
items.misconception: It halves — intensity is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged because the wave doesn't lose energy.
items.why: Students default to linear scaling instead of the inverse-square law that arises because the same energy spreads over a sphere of growing surface area.

items.correct: The driving frequency matches the natural frequency.
items.why: Correct answer: The driving frequency matches the natural frequency.. “Damping is critical.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Heavy damping in a car suspension causes:
items.correct: Slow return to equilibrium without oscillation.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Slow return to equilibrium without oscillation.. “Continuous oscillation at high amplitude.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
